During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, {{ill|Minamoto no Noriyori|ja|源範頼}}, visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}} during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kokura Domain|ja|小倉藩}} and received support from the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and later the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]]

According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kōsō Hachiman Shrine |location= 1-7-18 Kyūmoji, Moji Ward, Kitakyushu, Fukuoka Prefecture |position= {{Coord|33|57|10|N|130|58|04|E|region:JP-40_scale:30000|display=inline,title}} |deity= {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}
{{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命}} |rank= Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded= Jōgan 2 (860) |festival= October 14-15 |rituals= Mikoshi Procession (Ninashiki)
Hōjōe |image=[[File:Koso-Hachiman shrine.jpg|250px]]
Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社[[ja:甲宗八幡神社]]) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Moji Ward|ja|門司区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (first hall) * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from {{ill|Daian-ji|ja|大安寺}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山城国}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮}}. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, {{ill|Minamoto no Noriyori|ja|源範頼}}, visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}} during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kokura Domain|ja|小倉藩}} and received support from the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and later the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[category{Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:properly attributed pages]]

The shrine's former rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (first hall) * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from {{ill|Daian-ji|ja|大安寺}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山城国}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮}}. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, {{ill|Minamoto no Noriyori|ja|源範頼}}, visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}} during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kokura Domain|ja|小倉藩}} and received support from the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and later the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution 2]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:Pages needing attribution 2]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]]

The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:kk]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kōsō Hachiman Shrine |location= 1-7-18 Kyūmoji, Moji Ward, Kitakyushu, Fukuoka Prefecture |position= {{Coord|33|57|10|N|130|58|04|E|region:JP-40_scale:30000|display=inline,title}} |deity= {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}
{{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命}} |rank= Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded= Jōgan 2 (860) |festival= October 14-15 |rituals= Mikoshi Procession (Ninashiki)
Hōjōe |image=[[File:Koso-Hachiman shrine.jpg|250px]]
Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社[[ja:甲宗八幡神社]]) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Moji Ward|ja|門司区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (first hall) * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from {{ill|Daian-ji|ja|大安寺}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山城国}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮}}. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, {{ill|Minamoto no Noriyori|ja|源範頼}}, visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}} during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kokura Domain|ja|小倉藩}} and received support from the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and later the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:kk]]

Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社[[ja:甲宗八幡神社]]) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Moji Ward|ja|門司区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (first hall) * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from {{ill|Daian-ji|ja|大安寺}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山城国}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮}}. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, {{ill|Minamoto no Noriyori|ja|源範頼}}, visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}} during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kokura Domain|ja|小倉藩}} and received support from the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and later the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:kk]] {{Beppyo Shrines}}

The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kosou Hachimangu-qq]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:kk]] {{Beppyo Shrines}}

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kōsō Hachiman Shrine |location= 1-7-18 Kyūmoji, Moji Ward, Kitakyushu, Fukuoka Prefecture |position= {{Coord|33|57|10|N|130|58|04|E|region:JP-40_scale:30000|display=inline,title}} |deity= {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}
{{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命}} |rank= Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded= Jōgan 2 (860) |festival= October 14-15 |rituals= Mikoshi Procession (Ninashiki)
Hōjōe |image=[[File:Koso-Hachiman shrine.jpg|250px]]
Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社[[ja:甲宗八幡神社]]) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Moji Ward|ja|門司区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (first hall) * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from {{ill|Daian-ji|ja|大安寺}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山城国}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮}}. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, {{ill|Minamoto no Noriyori|ja|源範頼}}, visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}} during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kokura Domain|ja|小倉藩}} and received support from the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and later the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kosou Hachimangu-qq]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] {{Beppyo Shrines}}

The shrine's former rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (first hall) * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from {{ill|Daian-ji|ja|大安寺}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山城国}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮}}. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, {{ill|Minamoto no Noriyori|ja|源範頼}}, visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}} during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kokura Domain|ja|小倉藩}} and received support from the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and later the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kosou Hachimangu-qq]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] {{Beppyo Shrines}}

The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kosou Hachimangu-qq]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] {{Beppyo Shrines}}

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kōsō Hachiman Shrine |location= 1-7-18 Kyūmoji, Moji Ward, Kitakyushu, Fukuoka Prefecture |position= {{Coord|33|57|10|N|130|58|04|E|region:JP-40_scale:30000|display=inline,title}} |deity= {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}
{{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命}} |rank= Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded= Jōgan 2 (860) |festival= October 14-15 |rituals= Mikoshi Procession (Ninashiki)
Hōjōe |image=[[File:Koso-Hachiman shrine.jpg|250px]]
Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社[[ja:甲宗八幡神社]]) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Moji Ward|ja|門司区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (first hall) * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from {{ill|Daian-ji|ja|大安寺}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山城国}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮}}. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, {{ill|Minamoto no Noriyori|ja|源範頼}}, visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}} during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kokura Domain|ja|小倉藩}} and received support from the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and later the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kosou Hachimangu-qq]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:qq]] {{Beppyo Shrines}}

Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社[[ja:甲宗八幡神社]]) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Moji Ward|ja|門司区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (first hall) * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from {{ill|Daian-ji|ja|大安寺}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山城国}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮}}. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, {{ill|Minamoto no Noriyori|ja|源範頼}}, visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}} during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kokura Domain|ja|小倉藩}} and received support from the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and later the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kosou Hachimangu-qq]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:qq]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category:qq 1]]\[[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category:qq 2]]

In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kokura Domain|ja|小倉藩}} and received support from the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and later the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:Unsorted Completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kosou Hachimangu-qq]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:qq]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category:qq 1]]\[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kōsō Hachiman Shrine |location= 1-7-18 Kyūmoji, Moji Ward, Kitakyushu, Fukuoka Prefecture |position= {{Coord|33|57|10|N|130|58|04|E|region:JP-40_scale:30000|display=inline,title}} |deity= {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}
{{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命}} |rank= Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded= Jōgan 2 (860) |festival= October 14-15 |rituals= Mikoshi Procession (Ninashiki)
Hōjōe |image=[[File:Koso-Hachiman shrine.jpg|250px]]
Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社[[ja:甲宗八幡神社]]) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Moji Ward|ja|門司区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (first hall) * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from {{ill|Daian-ji|ja|大安寺}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山城国}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮}}. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, {{ill|Minamoto no Noriyori|ja|源範頼}}, visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}} during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kokura Domain|ja|小倉藩}} and received support from the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and later the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:Unsorted Completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kosou Hachimangu-qq]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kosou Hachimangu]][[Category:qq]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category:tier 2]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category:qq 1]]\[[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category:tier 2]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kōsō Hachiman Shrine |location= 1-7-18 Kyūmoji, Moji Ward, Kitakyushu, Fukuoka Prefecture |position= {{Coord|33|57|10|N|130|58|04|E|region:JP-40_scale:30000|display=inline,title}} |deity= {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}
{{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命}} |rank= Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded= Jōgan 2 (860) |festival= October 14-15 |rituals= Mikoshi Procession (Ninashiki)
Hōjōe |image=[[File:Koso-Hachiman shrine.jpg|250px]]
Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社[[ja:甲宗八幡神社]]) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Moji Ward|ja|門司区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (first hall) * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from {{ill|Daian-ji|ja|大安寺}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山城国}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮}}. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, {{ill|Minamoto no Noriyori|ja|源範頼}}, visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}} during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kokura Domain|ja|小倉藩}} and received support from the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and later the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}}
