Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Moji Ward|ja|門司区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|12=draft}}. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|12=draft}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft}}. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇|12=draft}} (first hall) * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft}} (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命|12=draft}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from {{ill|Daian-ji|ja|大安寺|12=draft}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}} (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教|12=draft}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮|12=draft}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成|12=draft}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|Yamashiro Province|12=draft}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮|12=draft}}. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山|12=draft}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, {{ill|Minamoto no Noriyori|ja|源範頼|12=draft}}, visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経|12=draft}} are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏|12=draft}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏|12=draft}} contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏|12=draft}} during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏|12=draft}}. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kokura Domain|ja|小倉藩|12=draft}} and received support from the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏|12=draft}} and later the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏|12=draft}}. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳|12=draft}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社|12=draft}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|12=draft}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区|12=draft}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川|12=draft}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛|12=draft}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[ja:甲宗八幡神社]] [[sn:Kosou Hachimangu]]

In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kokura Domain|ja|小倉藩|12=draft}} and received support from the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏|12=draft}} and later the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏|12=draft}}. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳|12=draft}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社|12=draft}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|12=draft}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区|12=draft}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川|12=draft}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛|12=draft}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[ja:甲宗八幡神社]] [[sn:Kosou Hachimangu]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kōsō Hachiman Shrine |location= 1-7-18 Kyūmoji, Moji Ward, Kitakyushu, Fukuoka Prefecture |position= {{Coord|33|57|10|N|130|58|04|E|region:JP-40_scale:30000|display=inline,title}} |deity= {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇|12=draft}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=draft}}
{{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理比売命|12=draft}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命|12=draft}} |rank= Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded= Jōgan 2 (860) |festival= October 14-15 |rituals= Mikoshi Procession (Ninashiki)
Hōjōe |image=[[File:Koso-Hachiman shrine.jpg|250px]]
Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Moji Ward|ja|門司区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|12=draft}}. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|12=draft}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft}}. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇|12=draft}} (first hall) * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft}} (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命|12=draft}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from {{ill|Daian-ji|ja|大安寺|12=draft}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}} (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教|12=draft}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮|12=draft}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成|12=draft}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|Yamashiro Province|12=draft}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮|12=draft}}. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山|12=draft}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, {{ill|Minamoto no Noriyori|ja|源範頼|12=draft}}, visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経|12=draft}} are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏|12=draft}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏|12=draft}} contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏|12=draft}} during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏|12=draft}}. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kokura Domain|ja|小倉藩|12=draft}} and received support from the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏|12=draft}} and later the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏|12=draft}}. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳|12=draft}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社|12=draft}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|12=draft}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区|12=draft}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川|12=draft}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛|12=draft}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[ja:甲宗八幡神社]] [[sn:Kosou Hachimangu]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kōsō Hachiman Shrine |location= 1-7-18 Kyūmoji, Moji Ward, Kitakyushu, Fukuoka Prefecture |position= {{Coord|33|57|10|N|130|58|04|E|region:JP-40_scale:30000|display=inline,title}} |deity= {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇|12=draft}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=draft}}
{{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理比売命|12=draft}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命|12=draft}} |rank= Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded= Jōgan 2 (860) |festival= October 14-15 |rituals= Mikoshi Procession (Ninashiki)
Hōjōe |image=[[File:Koso-Hachiman shrine.jpg|250px]]
Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Moji Ward|ja|門司区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|12=draft}}. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|12=draft}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft}}. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇|12=draft}} (first hall) * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft}} (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命|12=draft}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from {{ill|Daian-ji|ja|大安寺|12=draft}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}} (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教|12=draft}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮|12=draft}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成|12=draft}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|Yamashiro Province|12=draft}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮|12=draft}}. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山|12=draft}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, {{ill|Minamoto no Noriyori|ja|源範頼|12=draft}}, visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経|12=draft}} are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏|12=draft}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏|12=draft}} contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏|12=draft}} during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏|12=draft}}. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kokura Domain|ja|小倉藩|12=draft}} and received support from the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏|12=draft}} and later the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏|12=draft}}. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳|12=draft}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社|12=draft}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|12=draft}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区|12=draft}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川|12=draft}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛|12=draft}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:甲宗八幡神社]] [[sn:Kosou Hachimangu]]

Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Moji Ward|ja|門司区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|12=draft}}. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|12=draft}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft}}. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇|12=draft}} (first hall) * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft}} (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命|12=draft}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from {{ill|Daian-ji|ja|大安寺|12=draft}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}} (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教|12=draft}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮|12=draft}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成|12=draft}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|Yamashiro Province|12=draft}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮|12=draft}}. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山|12=draft}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, {{ill|Minamoto no Noriyori|ja|源範頼|12=draft}}, visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経|12=draft}} are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏|12=draft}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏|12=draft}} contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏|12=draft}} during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏|12=draft}}. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kokura Domain|ja|小倉藩|12=draft}} and received support from the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏|12=draft}} and later the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏|12=draft}}. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳|12=draft}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社|12=draft}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|12=draft}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区|12=draft}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川|12=draft}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛|12=draft}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:refreshing redirects]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[sn:Kosou Hachimangu]] }} [[ja:甲宗八幡神社]] [[sn:Kosou Hachimangu]]

During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区|12=draft}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川|12=draft}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛|12=draft}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:refreshing redirects]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Kosou Hachimangu]] [[ja:甲宗八幡神社]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kōsō Hachiman Shrine |location= 1-7-18 Kyūmoji, Moji Ward, Kitakyushu, Fukuoka Prefecture |position= {{Coord|33|57|10|N|130|58|04|E|region:JP-40_scale:30000|display=inline,title}} |deity= {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}
{{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命}} |Rank= Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded= Jōgan 2 (860) |festival= October 14-15 |rituals= Mikoshi Procession (Ninashiki)
Hōjōe |image=[[File:Koso-Hachiman shrine.jpg|250px]]
Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Moji Ward|ja|門司区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former Rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (first hall) * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from {{ill|Daian-ji|ja|大安寺}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province}} (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|Yamashiro Province}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮}}. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, {{ill|Minamoto no Noriyori|ja|源範頼}}, visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}} during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kokura Domain|ja|小倉藩}} and received support from the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and later the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:refreshing redirects]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Kosou Hachimangu]] [[ja:甲宗八幡神社]]

revision.comment: 31 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:甲宗八幡神社 up to rev 102019130
revision.text:
{{出典の明記| date = 2022-12}} {{神社 |名称= 甲宗八幡神社 |画像=[[画像:Koso-Hachiman shrine.jpg|260px|]]
拝殿 |所在地= [[福岡県]][[北九州市]][[門司区]]旧門司1丁目7-18 |位置= {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|57|10|N|130|58|04|E|region:JP-40_scale:30000|display=inline,title}} |祭神=[[応神天皇]]
[[神功皇后]]
[[市寸島比売命]]
[[多紀理比売命]]
[[タギツヒメ|多紀津比売命]] |社格=旧[[県社]]・[[別表神社]] |創建=[[貞観 (日本)|貞観]]2年（[[860年]]） |例祭=[[10月14日]]・[[10月15日|15日]] |神事=迎螺式（になしき）・放螺式（ほうじょうしき） |地図={{Location map|Japan Fukuoka|label=甲宗
八幡神社|coordinates= {{coord2|33|57|10|N|130|58|04|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|33|57|10|N|130|58|04|E}}}} }} '''甲宗八幡神社'''（こうそうはちまんじんじゃ）は、[[福岡県]][[北九州市]][[門司区]]にある[[神社]]。通称は'''甲宗八幡宮'''。江戸時代までは「門司社（甲宗社）」、「門司八幡宮（門司関八幡宮）」とも呼ばれた。旧[[社格]]は[[県社]]で、現在は[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]である。 == 祭神 == 第一殿に[[応神天皇]]、第二殿に[[神功皇后]]、第三殿に[[宗像三女神]]（[[イチキシマヒメ|市寸島比売命]]・[[タキリビメ|多紀理比売命]]・[[タギツヒメ|多紀津比売命]]）を祀る。 == 歴史 == [[貞観 (日本)|貞観]]元年（[[859年]]）、[[大和国]]（奈良県）[[大安寺]]の僧侶であった[[行教]]が[[宇佐神宮|宇佐八幡宮]]に参籠した折、「八幡神が王城を鎮護する」という神託が降ったため、翌貞観2年（[[860年]]）、[[清和天皇]]は太宰大弐・[[清原岑成]]を勅使として派遣し、八幡神を[[山城国]]（京都府）男山へ遷座する（後の石清水八幡宮）事となった。その途次、勅使一行が門司関にて霊峰と崇められていた筆立山の山麓に駐留すると、上空に瑞雲がたなびき八流の幡を天降すという瑞兆が現れ、行教は「大神の出現疑うべからず」と朝廷に上奏し、この瑞兆を喜んだ清和天皇の勅願によりこの地に八幡神を分祀し、併せて[[神功皇后]]着用の[[兜|甲]]（かぶと）を奉斎して創建されたのが始まりとされる。 以来、交通の要所である門司関の守護神として、平安時代末期においては[[寿永]]4年（[[1185年]]）に起こった[[壇ノ浦の戦い]]では鎌倉方の大将である[[源範頼]]が参詣し重藤弓を奉納して必勝を祈願し、その戦勝後には副将で弟である[[源義経]]とともに社殿を造営したと伝わる。[[鎌倉時代]]に入ると門司一帯を所領とした[[門司氏]]が氏神とし、門司六ヶ郷（楠原・柳・大積・伊川・吉志・片野）の総社的な扱いを受け、[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]初期の[[建武 (日本)|建武]]3年（[[1336年]]）には[[多々良浜の戦い]]に戦勝した[[足利尊氏]]より社殿造営および社領を寄進される等、武門の崇敬はますます高まった。[[室町時代|室町]][[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]においても西国の大名[[大内氏]]より代々篤い崇敬を受け、[[大内義興]]・[[大内義隆|義隆]]・[[大内義長|義長]]三代にわたって社領が寄進され、また大内氏の滅びた後に中国最大の大名となった[[毛利元就]]によっても社殿が造営されている。 近世に入ると社地は[[小倉藩]]に属し、[[細川氏]]、その後に[[小笠原氏]]の尊崇を受けた。特に小笠原藩政時代には藩内守護社（豊城鎮護社）の首座として歴代小倉藩主より重んじられ、例年の幣帛奉献はもちろん度々社殿造営および社領寄進が行われた。しかしながら幕末の[[長州征討|第二次長州征討]]の際、小倉藩内へ侵略した長州兵により本殿を焼き払われ神宝等も略奪の憂き目にあった。 [[明治]]元年（[[1868年]]）、[[毛利元徳]]により本殿が再建され、拝殿が小倉藩造、本殿が長州藩造の越境型社殿となる。その後、明治5年（[[1872年]]）に郷社に列し、[[大正]]11年（[[1922年]])には県社に昇格した。この間、[[門司港 (地区名)|門司港]]が全国有数の港町として発展したこともあり、大規模造営を行い、社殿・社務所の他に楼門・廻廊・奉幣殿・絵馬殿を備える県下有数規模の神社となったが、[[昭和]]20年（[[1945年]]）6月の門司を襲った空襲で全てを焼失した。 戦後、昭和33年（[[1958年]]）に神社の再建がなされ、神社本庁別表神社に列した。 == その他 == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|[[平知盛]]墓（左）同供養塔（右）]] * 特殊神事として50年に1度の式年大祭で神体（伝・神功皇后着用甲）の拝観が行われる。前々回は[[1958年]]（昭和33年）、前回は[[2008年]]（[[平成]]20年）に執り行われた。次回は[[2058年]]予定。 * 門司区でも最大の氏子区域を持つ神社として知られるが、本来の氏子区域はさらに広大で、小笠原時代以降に定められた氏子区域は、恒見地区を除く門司区全域、および小倉北区の足立・古船場町・馬借町・上城野水町周辺など、概ね現在の北九州市の[[紫川]]以東の地域に相当する。 * 境内には[[壇ノ浦の戦い]]で入水した[[平知盛]]の墓と伝えられる石塔がある。合戦後に流れ着いた知盛の遺体を里人が哀れみ、八幡宮が鎮座する筆立山の山中供養したのが始まりといわれる。戦後に境内地に移され今に至る。 * [[2010年]]（平成22年）[[1月16日]]、[[2011年]]（平成23年）[[1月15日]]に[[日本プロサッカーリーグ|J2リーグ]]の[[ギラヴァンツ北九州]]が必勝祈願を行なった[http://newwave-k.co.jp/news/index/0/391 ニューウェーブ北九州（ギラヴァンツ北九州）]最新ニュース [https://archive.is/20120713012009/mainichi.jp/seibu/sports/news/20100116ddg041050005000c.html 毎日新聞]オッショイ九州 - スポーツ。以降毎年選手が必勝祈願に参拝する。 == 神宝 == * 伝・神功皇后着用甲（神体） * 甲宗八幡宮文書（足利尊氏・大内義興・大内義隆文書等） == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * 『門司郷土叢書　第七巻　神社編・寺院編１』国書刊行会{{いつ|date=2022-12}} == 外部リンク == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ 甲宗八幡神社]公式ホームページ {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こうそうはちまんしんしや}} [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:福岡県の旧県社]] [[Category:北九州市の神社]] [[Category:門司区の歴史]] [[Category:門司区の建築物]] [[Category:八幡宮]]

(日本)|戦国時代]]においても西国の大名[[大内氏]]より代々篤い崇敬を受け、[[大内義興]]・[[大内義隆|義隆]]・[[大内義長|義長]]三代にわたって社領が寄進され、また大内氏の滅びた後に中国最大の大名となった[[毛利元就]]によっても社殿が造営されている。 近世に入ると社地は[[小倉藩]]に属し、[[細川氏]]、その後に[[小笠原氏]]の尊崇を受けた。特に小笠原藩政時代には藩内守護社（豊城鎮護社）の首座として歴代小倉藩主より重んじられ、例年の幣帛奉献はもちろん度々社殿造営および社領寄進が行われた。しかしながら幕末の[[長州征討|第二次長州征討]]の際、小倉藩内へ侵略した長州兵により本殿を焼き払われ神宝等も略奪の憂き目にあった。 [[明治]]元年（[[1868年]]）、[[毛利元徳]]により本殿が再建され、拝殿が小倉藩造、本殿が長州藩造の越境型社殿となる。その後、明治5年（[[1872年]]）に郷社に列し、[[大正]]11年（[[1922年]])には県社に昇格した。この間、[[門司港 (地区名)|門司港]]が全国有数の港町として発展したこともあり、大規模造営を行い、社殿・社務所の他に楼門・廻廊・奉幣殿・絵馬殿を備える県下有数規模の神社となったが、[[昭和]]20年（[[1945年]]）6月の門司を襲った空襲で全てを焼失した。 戦後、昭和33年（[[1958年]]）に神社の再建がなされ、神社本庁別表神社に列した。 == その他 == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|[[平知盛]]墓（左）同供養塔（右）]] * 特殊神事として50年に1度の式年大祭で神体（伝・神功皇后着用甲）の拝観が行われる。前々回は[[1958年]]（昭和33年）、前回は[[2008年]]（[[平成]]20年）に執り行われた。次回は[[2058年]]予定。 * 門司区でも最大の氏子区域を持つ神社として知られるが、本来の氏子区域はさらに広大で、小笠原時代以降に定められた氏子区域は、恒見地区を除く門司区全域、および小倉北区の足立・古船場町・馬借町・上城野水町周辺など、概ね現在の北九州市の[[紫川]]以東の地域に相当する。 * 境内には[[壇ノ浦の戦い]]で入水した[[平知盛]]の墓と伝えられる石塔がある。合戦後に流れ着いた知盛の遺体を里人が哀れみ、八幡宮が鎮座する筆立山の山中供養したのが始まりといわれる。戦後に境内地に移され今に至る。 * [[2010年]]（平成22年）[[1月16日]]、[[2011年]]（平成23年）[[1月15日]]に[[日本プロサッカーリーグ|J2リーグ]]の[[ギラヴァンツ北九州]]が必勝祈願を行なった[http://newwave-k.co.jp/news/index/0/391 ニューウェーブ北九州（ギラヴァンツ北九州）]最新ニュース [https://archive.is/20120713012009/mainichi.jp/seibu/sports/news/20100116ddg041050005000c.html 毎日新聞]オッショイ九州 - スポーツ。以降毎年選手が必勝祈願に参拝する。 == 神宝 == * 伝・神功皇后着用甲（神体） * 甲宗八幡宮文書（足利尊氏・大内義興・大内義隆文書等） == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * 『門司郷土叢書　第七巻　神社編・寺院編１』国書刊行会{{いつ|date=2022-12}} == 外部リンク == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ 甲宗八幡神社]公式ホームページ {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こうそうはちまんしんしや}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kōsō Hachiman Shrine |location= 1-7-18 Kyūmoji, Moji Ward, Kitakyushu, Fukuoka Prefecture |position= {{Coord|33|57|10|N|130|58|04|E|region:JP-40_scale:30000|display=inline,title}} |deity= {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}
{{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命}} |Rank= Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded= Jōgan 2 (860) |festival= October 14-15 |rituals= Mikoshi Procession (Ninashiki)
Hōjōe |image=[[File:Koso-Hachiman shrine.jpg|250px]]
Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Moji Ward|ja|門司区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former Rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (first hall) * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from {{ill|Daian-ji|ja|大安寺}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province}} (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|Yamashiro Province}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮}}. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, {{ill|Minamoto no Noriyori|ja|源範頼}}, visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}} during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kokura Domain|ja|小倉藩}} and received support from the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏}} and later the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏}}. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:refreshing redirects]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Kosou Hachimangu]] [[ja:甲宗八幡神社]] {{translated page|ja|甲宗八幡神社|version=102019130|comment=Imported full ja history}}

According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{translated page|ja|甲宗八幡神社|version=102019130|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:refreshing redirects]] }} [[sn:Kosou Hachimangu]] [[ja:甲宗八幡神社]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kōsō Hachiman Shrine |location= 1-7-18 Kyūmoji, Moji Ward, Kitakyushu, Fukuoka Prefecture |position= {{Coord|33|57|10|N|130|58|04|E|region:JP-40_scale:30000|display=inline,title}} |deity= {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇|12=draft}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft}}
{{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=draft}}
{{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理比売命|12=draft}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命|12=draft}} |Rank= Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded= Jōgan 2 (860) |festival= October 14-15 |rituals= Mikoshi Procession (Ninashiki)
Hōjōe |image=[[File:Koso-Hachiman shrine.jpg|250px]]
Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Moji Ward|ja|門司区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|12=draft}}. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former Rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|12=draft}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft}}. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇|12=draft}} (first hall) * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft}} (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命|12=draft}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from {{ill|Daian-ji|ja|大安寺|12=draft}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}} (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教|12=draft}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮|12=draft}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成|12=draft}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|Yamashiro Province|12=draft}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮|12=draft}}. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山|12=draft}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, {{ill|Minamoto no Noriyori|ja|源範頼|12=draft}}, visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経|12=draft}} are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏|12=draft}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏|12=draft}} contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏|12=draft}} during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏|12=draft}}. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kokura Domain|ja|小倉藩|12=draft}} and received support from the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏|12=draft}} and later the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏|12=draft}}. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳|12=draft}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社|12=draft}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|12=draft}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区|12=draft}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川|12=draft}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛|12=draft}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Kōsō Hachiman Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|甲宗八幡神社|version=102019130|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:refreshing redirects]] }} [[sn:Kosou Hachimangu]] [[ja:甲宗八幡神社]]

Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Moji Ward|ja|門司区|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|qq=draft}}. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former Rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社|1=Beppyo Shrine|2=ja|3=別表神社|4=simple|5=Beppyo shrine|6=zh|7=別表神社|8=mr|9=बेप्प्यो तीर्थ|qq=draft}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|qq=draft}}. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇|qq=draft}} (first hall) * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft}} (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命|qq=draft}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from {{ill|Daian-ji|ja|大安寺|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|qq=draft}} (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教|qq=draft}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮|qq=draft}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成|qq=draft}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|Yamashiro Province|qq=draft}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮|qq=draft}}. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山|qq=draft}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, {{ill|Minamoto no Noriyori|ja|源範頼|qq=draft}}, visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経|qq=draft}} are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏|qq=draft}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏|qq=draft}} contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏|qq=draft}} during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏|qq=draft}}. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kokura Domain|ja|小倉藩|qq=draft}} and received support from the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏|qq=draft}} and later the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏|qq=draft}}. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳|qq=draft}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社|qq=draft}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社|1=Beppyo Shrine|2=ja|3=別表神社|4=simple|5=Beppyo shrine|6=zh|7=別表神社|8=mr|9=बेप्प्यो तीर्थ|qq=draft}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区|qq=draft}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川|qq=draft}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛|qq=draft}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|甲宗八幡神社|version=102019130|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:refreshing redirects]] }} [[sn:Kosou Hachimangu]] [[ja:甲宗八幡神社]]

{{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}
{{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime|qq=}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=}} |Rank= Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded= Jōgan 2 (860) |festival= October 14-15 |rituals= Mikoshi Procession (Ninashiki)
Hōjōe |image=[[File:Koso-Hachiman shrine.jpg|250px]]
Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Moji Ward|ja|門司区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moji Ward|qq=}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu|qq=}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuoka Prefecture|qq=}}. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former Rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社|1=Beppyo Shrine|2=ja|3=別表神社|4=simple|5=Beppyo shrine|6=zh|7=別表神社|8=mr|9=बेप्प्यो तीर्थ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Special Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppyo Shrine|qq=}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Shinto Shrines|qq=}}. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ōjin|qq=}} (first hall) * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Jingū|qq=}} (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiribime|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from {{ill|Daian-ji|ja|大安寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daian-ji|qq=}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国|qq=Yamato Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Province|qq=}} (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōkyō|qq=}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Jingū|qq=}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Mitsunari|qq=}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|Yamashiro Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|qq=}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwashimizu Hachimangū|qq=}}. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudatetsu-yama|qq=}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, {{ill|Minamoto no Noriyori|ja|源範頼|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Noriyori|qq=}}, visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoshitsune|qq=}} are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moji clan|qq=}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashikaga Takauji|qq=}} contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōuchi clan|qq=}} during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mōri clan|qq=}}. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kokura Domain|ja|小倉藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokura Domain|qq=}} and received support from the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|ja|細川氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosokawa clan|qq=}} and later the {{ill|Ogasawara clan|ja|小笠原氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara clan|qq=}}. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mōri Motonori|qq=}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/District Shrine|qq=}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a {{ill|Special Shrine|ja|別表神社|1=Beppyo Shrine|2=ja|3=別表神社|4=simple|5=Beppyo shrine|6=zh|7=別表神社|8=mr|9=बेप्प्यो तीर्थ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Special Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppyo Shrine|qq=}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of {{ill|Kokurakita Ward|ja|小倉北区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokurakita Ward|qq=}}, covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasaki River|qq=}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|ja|平知盛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Tomomori|qq=}}, who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|甲宗八幡神社|version=102019130|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Moji Ward, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kosou Hachimangu]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Kosou Hachimangu]] [[ja:甲宗八幡神社]]
