{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11577728]] Wikidata name: Kōsō Hachiman Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11577728]] Wikidata name: Kōsō Hachiman Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kōsō Hachiman Shrine |location= 1-7-18 Kyūmoji, Moji Ward, Kitakyushu, Fukuoka Prefecture |position= {{Coord|33|57|10|N|130|58|04|E|region:JP-40_scale:30000|display=inline,title}} |deity= [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]]
[[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]]
{{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}
{{ill|Tagorihime|lt=Tagorihime|lt=Tagirihime|ja|多紀理比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} |Rank= Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded= Jōgan 2 (860) |festival= October 14-15 |rituals= Mikoshi Procession (Ninashiki)
Hōjōe |image=[[File:Koso-Hachiman shrine.jpg|250px]]
Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Moji-ku, Kitakyūshū|Moji Ward]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former Rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}, and it is currently listed as a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] by the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] (first hall) * [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, {{ill|Tagorihime|lt=Tagorihime|lt=Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiribime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from [[:en:Daian-ji|Daian-ji]] in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōkyō|qq=|1=Gyōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626127}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Jingū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇佐神宮}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Mitsunari|qq=|1=Kiyohara no Mitsunari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11560502}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|Yamashiro Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]]. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudatetsu-yama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, [[:en:Minamoto no Noriyori|Minamoto no Noriyori]], visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moji clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]] during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Kokura Domain|Kokura Domain]] and received support from the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and later the [[:en:Ogasawara clan|Ogasawara clan]]. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mōri Motonori|qq=|1=Mōri Motonori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547542}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/District Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita Ward]], covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasaki River|qq=|1=Murasaki River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605719}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of [[:en:Taira no Tomomori|Taira no Tomomori]], who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|甲宗八幡神社|version=102019130|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Kosou Hachimangu]] [[ja:甲宗八幡神社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Moji-ku, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward]] {{Commons category|Koso Hachiman Shrine}}

Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Moji-ku, Kitakyūshū|Moji Ward]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former Rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}, and it is currently listed as a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] by the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] (first hall) * [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, {{ill|Tagorihime|lt=Tagorihime|lt=Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiribime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from [[:en:Daian-ji|Daian-ji]] in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōkyō|qq=|1=Gyōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626127}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Jingū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇佐神宮}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Mitsunari|qq=|1=Kiyohara no Mitsunari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11560502}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|Yamashiro Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]]. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudatetsu-yama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, [[:en:Minamoto no Noriyori|Minamoto no Noriyori]], visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moji clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]] during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Kokura Domain|Kokura Domain]] and received support from the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and later the [[:en:Ogasawara clan|Ogasawara clan]]. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mōri Motonori|qq=|1=Mōri Motonori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547542}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|qid=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/District Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita Ward]], covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasaki River|qq=|1=Murasaki River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605719}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of [[:en:Taira no Tomomori|Taira no Tomomori]], who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|甲宗八幡神社|version=102019130|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Kosou Hachimangu]] [[ja:甲宗八幡神社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Moji-ku, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward]] {{Commons category|Koso Hachiman Shrine}} {{wikidata link|Q11577728}}

In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Kokura Domain|Kokura Domain]] and received support from the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and later the [[:en:Ogasawara clan|Ogasawara clan]]. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mōri Motonori|qq=|1=Mōri Motonori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547542}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|qid=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/District Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita Ward]], covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasaki River|qq=|1=Murasaki River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605719}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of [[:en:Taira no Tomomori|Taira no Tomomori]], who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|甲宗八幡神社|version=102019130|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:甲宗八幡神社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Moji-ku, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward]] {{Commons category|Koso Hachiman Shrine}} {{wikidata link|Q11577728}}

Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Moji-ku, Kitakyūshū|Moji Ward]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former Rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}, and it is currently listed as a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] by the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] (first hall) * [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, {{ill|Tagorihime|lt=Tagorihime|lt=Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiribime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from [[:en:Daian-ji|Daian-ji]] in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōkyō|qq=|1=Gyōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626127}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Jingū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇佐神宮}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Mitsunari|qq=|1=Kiyohara no Mitsunari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11560502}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|Yamashiro Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]]. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudatetsu-yama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, [[:en:Minamoto no Noriyori|Minamoto no Noriyori]], visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moji clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]] during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Kokura Domain|Kokura Domain]] and received support from the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and later the [[:en:Ogasawara clan|Ogasawara clan]]. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mōri Motonori|qq=|1=Mōri Motonori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547542}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|qid=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/District Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita Ward]], covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasaki River|qq=|1=Murasaki River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605719}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of [[:en:Taira no Tomomori|Taira no Tomomori]], who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|甲宗八幡神社|version=102019130|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:甲宗八幡神社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Moji-ku, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward]] {{Commons category|Koso Hachiman Shrine}} {{wikidata link|Q11577728}}

Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Moji-ku, Kitakyūshū|Moji Ward]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former Rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}, and it is currently listed as a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] by the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] (first hall) * [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, {{ill|Tagorihime|lt=Tagorihime|lt=Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiribime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from [[:en:Daian-ji|Daian-ji]] in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōkyō|qq=|1=Gyōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626127}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Jingū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇佐神宮}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Mitsunari|qq=|1=Kiyohara no Mitsunari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11560502}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|Yamashiro Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]]. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudatetsu-yama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, [[:en:Minamoto no Noriyori|Minamoto no Noriyori]], visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moji clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]] during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Kokura Domain|Kokura Domain]] and received support from the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and later the [[:en:Ogasawara clan|Ogasawara clan]]. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mōri Motonori|qq=|1=Mōri Motonori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547542}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/District Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita Ward]], covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasaki River|qq=|1=Murasaki River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605719}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of [[:en:Taira no Tomomori|Taira no Tomomori]], who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|甲宗八幡神社|version=102019130|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:甲宗八幡神社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Moji-ku, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward]] {{Commons category|Koso Hachiman Shrine}} {{wikidata link|Q11577728}}

The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former Rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}, and it is currently listed as a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] by the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] (first hall) * [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, {{ill|Tagorihime|lt=Tagorihime|lt=Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiribime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from [[:en:Daian-ji|Daian-ji]] in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōkyō|1=Gyōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626127}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Jingū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇佐神宮}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Mitsunari|1=Kiyohara no Mitsunari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11560502}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|Yamashiro Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]]. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudatetsu-yama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, [[:en:Minamoto no Noriyori|Minamoto no Noriyori]], visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moji clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]] during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Kokura Domain|Kokura Domain]] and received support from the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and later the [[:en:Ogasawara clan|Ogasawara clan]]. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mōri Motonori|1=Mōri Motonori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547542}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/District Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita Ward]], covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasaki River|1=Murasaki River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605719}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of [[:en:Taira no Tomomori|Taira no Tomomori]], who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|甲宗八幡神社|version=102019130|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:甲宗八幡神社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Moji-ku, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward]] {{Commons category|Koso Hachiman Shrine}} {{wikidata link|Q11577728}}

In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/District Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita Ward]], covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasaki River|1=Murasaki River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605719}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of [[:en:Taira no Tomomori|Taira no Tomomori]], who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|甲宗八幡神社|version=102019130|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:甲宗八幡神社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Moji-ku, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward]] {{Commons category|Koso Hachiman Shrine}} {{wikidata link|Q11577728}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Moji-ku, Kitakyūshū|Moji Ward]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former Rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}, and it is currently listed as a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] by the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] (first hall) * [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, {{ill|Tagorihime|lt=Tagorihime|lt=Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiribime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from [[:en:Daian-ji|Daian-ji]] in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōkyō|1=Gyōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626127}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Jingū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇佐神宮}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Mitsunari|1=Kiyohara no Mitsunari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11560502}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|Yamashiro Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]]. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudatetsu-yama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, [[:en:Minamoto no Noriyori|Minamoto no Noriyori]], visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moji clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]] during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Kokura Domain|Kokura Domain]] and received support from the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and later the [[:en:Ogasawara clan|Ogasawara clan]]. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mōri Motonori|1=Mōri Motonori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547542}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/District Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita Ward]], covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasaki River|1=Murasaki River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605719}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of [[:en:Taira no Tomomori|Taira no Tomomori]], who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|甲宗八幡神社|version=102019130|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:甲宗八幡神社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Moji-ku, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward]] {{Commons category|Koso Hachiman Shrine}} {{wikidata link|Q11577728}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Moji-ku, Kitakyūshū|Moji Ward]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former Rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}, and it is currently listed as a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] by the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] (first hall) * [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, {{ill|Tagorihime|lt=Tagorihime|lt=Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiribime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from [[:en:Daian-ji|Daian-ji]] in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōkyō|1=Gyōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626127}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Jingū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇佐神宮}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Mitsunari|1=Kiyohara no Mitsunari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11560502}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|Yamashiro Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]]. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudatetsu-yama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, [[:en:Minamoto no Noriyori|Minamoto no Noriyori]], visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moji clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]] during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Kokura Domain|Kokura Domain]] and received support from the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and later the [[:en:Ogasawara clan|Ogasawara clan]]. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mōri Motonori|1=Mōri Motonori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547542}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/District Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita Ward]], covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasaki River|1=Murasaki River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605719}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of [[:en:Taira no Tomomori|Taira no Tomomori]], who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|甲宗八幡神社|version=102019130|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:甲宗八幡神社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Moji-ku, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward]] {{Commons category|Koso Hachiman Shrine}} {{wikidata link|Q11577728}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]] during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Kokura Domain|Kokura Domain]] and received support from the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and later the [[:en:Ogasawara clan|Ogasawara clan]]. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mōri Motonori|1=Mōri Motonori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547542}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/District Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita Ward]], covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasaki River|1=Murasaki River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605719}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of [[:en:Taira no Tomomori|Taira no Tomomori]], who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|甲宗八幡神社|version=102019130|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:甲宗八幡神社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Moji-ku, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward]] {{Commons category|Koso Hachiman Shrine}} {{wikidata link|Q11577728}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

{{ill|Tagorihime|lt=Tagorihime|lt=Tagirihime|ja|多紀理比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}}

Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Moji-ku, Kitakyūshū|Moji Ward]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former Rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}, and it is currently listed as a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] by the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] (first hall) * [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, {{ill|Tagorihime|lt=Tagorihime|lt=Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiribime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from [[:en:Daian-ji|Daian-ji]] in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōkyō|1=Gyōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626127}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Jingū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇佐神宮}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Mitsunari|1=Kiyohara no Mitsunari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11560502}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|Yamashiro Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]]. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudatetsu-yama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, [[:en:Minamoto no Noriyori|Minamoto no Noriyori]], visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moji clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]] during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Kokura Domain|Kokura Domain]] and received support from the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and later the [[:en:Ogasawara clan|Ogasawara clan]]. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mōri Motonori|1=Mōri Motonori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547542}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/District Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita Ward]], covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasaki River|1=Murasaki River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605719}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of [[:en:Taira no Tomomori|Taira no Tomomori]], who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|甲宗八幡神社|version=102019130|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:甲宗八幡神社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Moji-ku, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward]] {{Commons category|Koso Hachiman Shrine}} {{wikidata link|Q11577728}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

Haiden }} '''Kōsō Hachiman Shrine''' (甲宗八幡神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Moji-ku, Kitakyūshū|Moji Ward]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]. The shrine is also known by its common name, Kōsō Hachiman-gū. During the Edo period, it was referred to as "Moji Shrine (Kōsō Shrine)" or "Moji Hachiman-gū (Moji Seki Hachiman-gū)." The shrine's former Rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}, and it is currently listed as a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] by the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are: * [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] (first hall) * [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] (second hall) * {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, {{ill|Tagorihime|lt=Tagorihime|lt=Takiribime|ja|多紀理比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiribime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}}, and {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多紀津比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (third hall) == History == In Jōgan 1 (859), a monk from [[:en:Daian-ji|Daian-ji]] in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] (Nara Prefecture), {{ill|Gyōkyō|ja|行教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōkyō|1=Gyōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626127}}, prayed at {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Jingū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇佐神宮}} and received a divine message stating that the Hachiman deity would protect the royal capital. The following year, in Jōgan 2 (860), Emperor Seiwa dispatched an imperial envoy, {{ill|Kiyohara no Mitsunari|ja|清原岑成|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Mitsunari|1=Kiyohara no Mitsunari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11560502}}, to transfer the Hachiman deity to Otokoyama in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|Yamashiro Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Kyoto Prefecture), where it was later enshrined at what became [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]]. During their journey, the imperial envoy stopped at the base of {{ill|Fudatetsu-yama|ja|筆立山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudatetsu-yama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where a miraculous cloud appeared, indicating the presence of the deity. Gyōkyō reported this to the court, and the delighted Emperor Seiwa ordered the enshrinement of the Hachiman deity at this location, along with the helmet (kabuto) worn by Empress Jingū. This marked the founding of the shrine. Since then, Kōsō Hachiman Shrine has been regarded as the guardian of Moji Seki, a strategic point of passage. During the Battle of Dan-no-ura in 1185, the commander of the Kamakura forces, [[:en:Minamoto no Noriyori|Minamoto no Noriyori]], visited the shrine, dedicated a yumi (bow), and prayed for victory. After the victory, Noriyori and his brother [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] are said to have rebuilt the shrine. During the Kamakura period, the {{ill|Moji clan|ja|門司氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moji clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} who controlled the area regarded the shrine as their tutelary deity. In the early Nanboku-chō period (1336), [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] contributed to the reconstruction of the shrine and donated lands to it. The shrine continued to receive patronage from various warlords, including the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]] during the Muromachi and Sengoku periods, and later the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the Edo period, the shrine was under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Kokura Domain|Kokura Domain]] and received support from the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and later the [[:en:Ogasawara clan|Ogasawara clan]]. During the second Chōshū expedition in the late Edo period, the shrine was burned down by Chōshū forces. It was rebuilt in 1868 by {{ill|Mōri Motonori|ja|毛利元徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mōri Motonori|1=Mōri Motonori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547542}} and continued to receive patronage. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|District Shrine|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/District Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1872 and was later elevated to {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} status in 1922. During this time, Moji Port became one of Japan's leading port towns, and the shrine was expanded with additional buildings, including a tower gate, corridors, and various halls. However, it was completely destroyed during an air raid in 1945. The shrine was rebuilt in 1958 and is now a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Notable Features == [[File:Tairanotomomorihaka.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Tomb of Taira no Tomomori (left) and Memorial Tower (right)]] * The shrine features a ritual every 50 years where the sacred treasures (said to be the helmet of Empress Jingū) are displayed to the public. The last events were in 1958 and 2008, with the next scheduled for 2058. * The shrine's original parish included the entire Moji Ward and parts of [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita Ward]], covering a vast area east of the {{ill|Murasaki River|ja|紫川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasaki River|1=Murasaki River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605719}}. * The shrine grounds contain a tomb believed to be that of [[:en:Taira no Tomomori|Taira no Tomomori]], who died in the Battle of Dan-no-ura. According to tradition, his body washed ashore and was buried here by locals. == annual Events == * **October 14-15:** festival * **July:** Tobata Gion Yamakasa Event * **Ninashiki and Hōjōe:** Special rituals held throughout the year == Notable Artifacts == * The helmet of Empress Jingū (sacred treasure) * Documents from Ashikaga Takauji, Ōuchi Yoshioki, Ōuchi Yoshitaka, and other historical figures == External Links == * [http://www.kosohachimangu.jp/ Kōsō Hachiman Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|甲宗八幡神社|version=102019130|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:甲宗八幡神社]] {{Commons category|Koso Hachiman Shrine}} {{wikidata link|Q11577728}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings in Moji-ku, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:History of Moji Ward]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
